content:
===accordion
![Indominus Rex, Alpha](https://i.ibb.co/8mbN5tX/AE5-E2935-93-CA-4900-AC57-32091-DDF5-D6-B.jpg)
===panel: Deck Name Origin
===center
## Skip to 3:05 😉
===endcenter
[[youtube:tXuU4d4CHfM?si=jYRtc19n1PEZMxkX&t=185]]
===endpanel
===panel: Theme
**Significant update (9/04/25):** I have moved Indominus further away from collecting keywords. This new build focuses on +1/+1 counter synergies and can function without Indominus in play. This new build has been significantly more explosive and is now capable of early wins (t4/5). Bear with me as I update the primer and continue to optimize Indominus for bracket 4! 


[[Indominus Rex, Alpha]] is a highly tuned and ***Optimized*** [[symbol:u]][[symbol:b]][[symbol:g]] deck that focuses on +1/+1 counter synergies and utilizing her ability to gain multiple keyword abilities. By leveraging powerful card advantage engines, efficient interaction, and premium tutors to establish and maintain parity while working towards various win conditions including commander damage and layered combos.
===endpanel
===panel: Game-plan
This deck revolves around +1/+1 counter synergies and leverages [[Indominus Rex, Alpha]]’s ability to gain multiple keyword abilities and draw cards based on the number of counters. The strategy no longer involves discarding mediocre creatures to enhance Indominus. `(check out Probabilities and Expected Value section for more details on bracket 2/3 Indominus builds)`

**Early Mana & Setup (Turns 1-2):** 

Prioritize deploying early mana like [[Devoted Druid]], [[Gyre Sage]], [[Incubation Druid]], [[Kami of Whispered Hopes]], and [[Crystalline Crawler]]. Aim to establish a strong mana base and build towards assembling counter engines. 

**Counter Synergies & Board Development (Turns 2-4):** 

Begin deploying +1/+1 counter enablers  like [[Arwen, Weaver of Hope]], [[Warden of the Grove]], [[Master Biomancer]], and [[Ouroboroid]]. These cards rapidly scale your creatures, turning even small mana dorks into significant threats.

**Commander Deployment & Value (Turns 4-6):** 

Cast Indominus Rex, Alpha. Maximize her ETB trigger by discarding creatures with multiple keywords (e.g. Breaker of Creation, Sire of Seven Deaths) and leveraging the ETB +1/+1 counter enablers, to draw a large number of cards. This reloads your hand and provides the fuel necessary to proceed to closing the game.

**Win Condition Execution (Turns 4+):**

Overwhelming Combat: With numerous counter doublers and large threats creating an unmanageable board state. Creatures like [[Sire of Seven Deaths]], and [[Vorinclex, Monstrous Raider ]] or a massive, keyword-laden Indominus Rex can quickly eliminate opponents through combat damage. The commander damage clock can rapidly change with cards like [[Proft's Eidetic Memory]] and [[Unspeakable Symbol]]. 

Infinite Turns: Assemble [[Sage of Hours]] with a reliable way to put five or more +1/+1 counters on it repeatedly.

Infinite Mana: [[Incubation Druid]] + [[Agatha's Soul Cauldron]] (exiling [[Devoted Druid]]) combo generates infinite mana. 
`(Check out the section below)`

The deck includes numerous utility creatures with relevant abilities, as well as powerful spells for interaction and card advantage. The deck typically wins through commander damaging the largest threat with little notice and then cleaning up after asserting control with overwhelming card advantage. 


===accordion|autocollapse
===panel: Infinite Mana
![[Infinite Mana]](https://commanderspellbook.com/api/combo/3990-4613-4762/generate-image)
![[Infinite Mana 2]](https://commanderspellbook.com/api/combo/4762-5455/generate-image)
![[Infinite Mana 3]](https://commanderspellbook.com/api/combo/2060-5455/generate-image)
===endpanel
===panel: Infinite Turns (Sage of Hours)
![Infinite Turns 1](https://commanderspellbook.com/api/combo/1494-6599/generate-image)
![Infinite Turns 2](https://commanderspellbook.com/api/combo/1494-6755/generate-image)
![Infinite Turns 3](https://commanderspellbook.com/api/combo/1494-6670/generate-image)
===endpanel
===endaccordion
You will notice that I have modified the casting cost of [[Breaker of Creation]] and [[Sire of Seven Deaths]] to [[symbol:0]]. This is a result of how Indominus plays, where these creatures are almost always worse to cast than to save in hand to pitch for Indominus later. They are effectively [[symbol:infinity-c]] or [[symbol:0]] mana draw 5-6. Hard choice on how to denote this in the mana curve of the deck...
===endpanel
===panel: Power Level
This Optimized (bracket 4) deck demonstrates a well-tuned strategy with powerful cards and synergies, placing it in the high power level range. Its consistency is high due to significant card draw, while its interaction suite allows it to disrupt opponents effectively. While it lacks most of the explosive fast mana typical of higher-powered decks, it makes up for this with efficient tutors, premium interaction, and strong value engines. 

The deck's speed is moderate, typically aiming to establish a strong board presence by turns 5-6. It's more resilient and consistent than a Focused deck but lacks the explosive speed and efficiency of cEDH decks. The inclusion of powerful cards and strategies push it above Upgraded into Optimized (High Power).

===endpanel
===panel: Weaknesses
This deck may struggle against fast combo decks that can win before it sets up its strategy. It's also vulnerable to strategies which deny draw i.e. [[Narset, Parter of Veils]] or punish draw i.e. [[Nekusar, the Mindrazer]], which can disrupt its main game plan. Indominus is also particularly susceptible to board wipes given her creature centric game-plan. 
===endpanel
===panel: Signature Cards
- [[Sage of Hours]]
- [[Proft's Eidetic Memory]]
- [[Crystalline Crawler]]
- [[Unspeakable Symbol]]
- [[Sultai Banner]]

Sultai Banner was (05/2025) a flavor inclusion aligning to [[user:jesusej_2072]] designs but was replaced with [[The Warring Triad]]. See his comment below for more insight:

”Just thought we’d drop in to comment on [[Sultai Banner]] — specifically, aligning to conversations with my brother prior to his decision to include it here. Love it — by the way.

First, the card offers beautiful artistic expression accompanied* by (what-seems-to-me) amazing flavor text for Indominus — in addition to embodying important Earthly semiotics, where roots run deep with supporting historical context.

A question: while [[Arcane Signet]] & [[Commander’s Sphere]] may be objectively more efficient — what impact does this have on gameplay, and how might we measure shifts in observable outcomes related to this specific design decision?

A fact: all mana rocks that are not mana positive remain value-adding across turns 1-3 — with a significantly depreciating return-on-investment after turn 3, which includes dead draws in all late-game scenerios.

A thought: folks run sub-optimal “Plan Cards” in almost every design we review, including our designs! Yet, we tend to focus on low-hanging-fruit because it’s easier to conceptualize & understand — weird, right?

And a hypothesis: in cases where design is tightly layered favoring “cohesion” — including one suboptimal mana rock is unlikely to have any observable impact on gameplay and related outcomes in Bracket 4, possibly even Bracket 5. Certainly less-so than lousy plans, poor layering, and less-than-optimal mulligan decisions.

And a final question: what is your favorite piece* of art across Magic: the Gathering — and why?

Hope this is evocative, while being an interesting thought experiment!

— ✌️😇”
===endpanel
===panel: Notable Exclusions
- [[Thassa’s Oracle]] | [[Demonic Consultation]] 
- [[Food Chain]] | [[Misthollow Griffin]]
- [[Devoted Druid]] | [[Hazel's Brewmaster]]

These combos, while increasing the decks power level, feel like crossing the line into cEDH territory. The deck will no longer be an Indominus deck, it will morph into a Sultai cEDH list helmed by a sub-optimal commander. Therefore, I have intentionally excluded these combos from the deck. I think this choice is the clear delineation between Indominus as bracket 4 vs bracket 5. 
===endpanel
===panel: Relevant MTG Rules
**702.1c** `An effect may state that “the same is true for” a list of keyword abilities or similar. If one of those keyword abilities has variants or variables and the effect grants that keyword or counters of that keyword to one or more objects and/or players, it grants each appropriate variant and variable of that keyword.`

**702.11f** `“Hexproof from [quality A] and from [quality B]” is shorthand for “hexproof from [quality A]” and “hexproof from \[quality B\]”; it behaves as two separate hexproof abilities. If an effect causes an object with such an ability to lose hexproof from [quality A], for example, that object would still have hexproof from [quality B].`

===endpanel
===panel: Probabilities and Expected Value
These statistics were calculated on the deck
as of 01/2025. The quantity of each keyword in the deck will continue to fluctuate with the ultimate goal being to increase the overall power level. The underlying principles still hold though. The calculations shift significantly with more tutors and any duplicated keywords from [[Sire of Seven Deaths]] have no impact. 

**Update 1 (3/14/25):** I have added more tutors in lieu of most of the bad creatures. Goals being:  
* Find [[Sire of Seven Deaths]] and [[Breaker of Creation]] to draw 11 baseline. 
* Early creatures for board presence are important in some matchups. 
    1. Mana curve may look fine but when you keep all of the keywords in hand for discarding later, you might just lose after casting Indominus due to your low life total.
    2. Early disposable creatures limit Indominus’ exposure to sacrifice effects.
* Tutors have impact when drawn after Indominus is already on the Battlefield. Drawing 3-5 keyword soup creatures off Indominus etb feels awful in high power games. 😅

**Update 2 (9/04/25):** I have moved away from a commander centric game-plan. The main issues I encountered in Update 1:
* It was nearly impossible to ramp, tutor for specific keyword creatures (mostly a waste of good tutors), maintain a board presence, and hold up interaction for the faster decks. This manifested as Indominus playing pod police and feeling clumsy without ever getting off the ground. 
* Bracket 4 decks Indominus routinely plays with easily bypass her keyword protections and she then is stranded in the command zone until I reassemble some keywords. 

I expect that **Update 2** will address some of these issues highlighted from my last few months of experiential results.  
===accordion
===panel: Keyword Dependency Map
![Keyword Dependency Map](https://i.ibb.co/FJrTxZV/IMG-5544.png)
===center
**Link:** [Keyword Dependency Visualization](https://app.everviz.com/embed/FkjGmxbZo/?v=6)
===endcenter
===endpanel
===panel: Base Probabilities & Expected Value
**Below is a single table summarizing:**
1. The approximate probability of seeing at least one creature with each keyword by Turn 3, Turn 4, and Turn 5.
2. The total expected number of distinct keywords (the sum of those probabilities) at the bottom of each column.

===center
	*Note: These probabilities use the binomial approximation for simplicity. (Exact hypergeometric values would be close.)*
===endcenter

![Probabilities](https://i.ibb.co/QbyMQb1/IMG-5548.jpg)

**Interpretation:**
* Each cell in the body of the table (i.e. 0.40 for Menace at Turn 3) is the approximate probability (40%) that your first 10 drawn cards include at least one Menace creature.
* At the bottom (Sum row), adding these probabilities gives the expected number of unique keywords you’ll see among your drawn creatures by that turn.
* For instance, by Turn 5, you can expect to have around 5.45 distinct keywords in hand.

This provides a handy snapshot of how likely each keyword is to appear, as well as the average total variety of keyword abilities given and cards drawn. These calculations ignore the presence of tutors and early card draw for simplicity…
===endpanel
===panel: Considering Tutors
**The Tutor Scenario:** 

Fetching [[Sire of Seven Deaths]] (SoSD)

SoSD has 6 keywords by itself:
* Menace, Vigilance, First Strike, Lifelink, Reach, and Trample.

That means if you do draw a tutor in time, you can guarantee at least those 6 keywords. Then, you might still draw other creatures that have some of the 5 remaining keywords (SoSD is missing Deathtouch, Flying, Haste, Hexproof, Indestructible).

## Turn 4: If Tutor Is Drawn by Turn 3
* By Turn 4, you see 11 total cards.
* Scenario:
1. Among the first 10 you found a tutor, you cast it, and you fetched SoSD.
2. That ensures you have all 6 keywords from SoSD.
3. You then see 1 more card on Turn 4 (your 11th card).

Hence, out of those 11 cards:
* 1 is your tutor (already used to fetch SoSD).
* 10 are other random draws from the deck.

## Probability of the 5 “Missing” Keywords

The 5 keywords not on SoSD are Deathtouch, Flying, Haste, Hexproof, Indestructible. 
* For n(w)=5 (Deathtouch, Haste, Hexproof, Indestructible), we get about 0.34 each.
* For n(w)=13 (Flying), we get about 0.57.

Summing these for the “missing five”:

0.34 + 0.57 + 0.34 + 0.34 + 0.34 = 1.93.

That 1.93 is the expected additional distinct keywords from those 5 categories.

## Weighted Average for Turn 4

Putting it all together with the 40% chance of having a tutor by Turn 3:
![Wieghted T4 Avg](https://i.ibb.co/RQ267sZ/IMG-5549.jpg)
So your expected number of distinct keywords by Turn 4 is now 6.25, once we account for the possibility of tutoring SoSD in time.

## Turn 5: If Tutor Is Drawn by Turn 4
* By Turn 5, you see 12 total cards.
* Scenario:
1. In the first 11 cards (by Turn 4), you found a tutor.
2. You fetch SoSD.
3. Then you see 1 more card on Turn 5, making a total of 12.

Within those 12 cards:
* 1 is your tutor -> SoSD
* 11 are random draws.

## The 5 Missing Keywords Among 11 Random Cards

Again, we check Deathtouch, Flying, Haste, Hexproof, Indestructible:

* For n(w)=5, that’s about 0.40 each.
* For n(w)=13 (Flying), that’s about 0.68.

Summing those 5:

0.40 + 0.68 + 0.40 + 0.40 + 0.40 = 2.28.

So if you tutor up SoSD by Turn 4, you expect:

6 (SoSD) + 2.28 (Others) = 8.28 (unique keywords)

## Weighted Average for Turn 5
![Weighted T5 Avg](https://i.ibb.co/Cw1q0t0/IMG-5550.jpg)

## Final Results

Here’s a concise table showing the expected number of distinct keywords by Turn 4 and Turn 5, once we factor in:
* The baseline scenario (no tutor drawn in time).
* The tutor scenario (if drawn by Turn 3 for T4 or by Turn 4 for T5).
* The probability of each scenario.
![Expected Value Table for Tutors](https://i.ibb.co/mb4grdQ/IMG-5552.jpg)
**Interpretation**
* By Turn 4, you’ll see on average 6.25 distinct keywords among your creatures, thanks to a ~40% chance of tutoring up [[Sire of Seven Deaths]] by Turn 3.
* By Turn 5, that rises to 6.67 distinct keywords on average, since you have ~43% to find a tutor by Turn 4 and fetch [[Sire of Seven Deaths]] (potentially netting you 8+ keywords).

In short, adding the 5 creature tutors (and being able to cast them early) boosts your expected keyword count from the 5–5.5 range into the 6–7 range, greatly enhancing [[Indominus Rex, Alpha]]’s resilience and card draw. 
===endpanel
===panel: Effects of Card Draw
## Approximate “Extra Cards Seen” by Turn 3

A quick way to estimate the effect of these draw spells is via linearity of expectation:
1. Each draw spell has some chance of appearing in your first 3-turn hand (normally 10 cards, but we’ll call that 10 for reference).
2. If a “draw 1” card appears, that’s +1 card seen. A “draw 2” is +2, etc.
3. We sum those expected values to estimate the average total “extra” cards seen by Turn 3.

By rough calculation (binomial‐style), you might end up seeing about +1 to +2 extra cards, on average, by Turn 3 (sometimes none, sometimes chaining into more).

In other words, instead of effectively seeing only 10 cards by Turn 3, you might see 11 or 12 on average.

## Revised “Effective” Card Counts

To keep it tractable, let’s assume:
* Turn 3: You effectively see 11 or 12 cards (instead of 10).
* Turn 4: Therefore you’ve effectively seen 12-13 cards total.
* Turn 5: You’ve effectively seen 13-14 cards total.

This bumps up every probability of drawing a key creature or tutor by a noticeable margin.

## Key Pieces to Fetch or Draw Early
1. [[Sire of Seven Deaths]]
* Has 6 different keywords (Menace, Vigilance, First Strike, Lifelink, Reach, Trample).
* If you see it in the first few turns, you’re already +6 distinct keywords!
2. Next-Best Multi-Keyword Creature
* Because SoSD lacks Deathtouch, Flying, Haste, Hexproof, Indestructible, you want a second creature that covers some of those.
* i.e. [[Nightveil Predator]] (NP) has Deathtouch, Flying, Hexproof.
* None of these 3 overlap with SoSD’s 6, so SoSD + NP combined = 9 unique keywords!
3. Five Tutors
* If you don’t draw SoSD naturally, you’ll want to tutor for it.
* If you already have SoSD in hand by Turn 3, your tutor fetches the next best multi-keyword creature (e.g. NP for 3 new keywords).

## Scenario Breakdown by Turn 4 (Illustration)

Let’s define an “effective Turn 3” where you’ve seen ~11–12 cards thanks to early draw spells. Then by Turn 4, you’ll have seen ~12–13 total. We consider four main scenarios:
1. You draw SoSD but no tutor
* SoSD alone gives you 6 keywords.
* Your other draws may provide some or all of the 5 missing keywords (Deathtouch, Flying, Haste, Hexproof, Indestructible).
2. You draw a tutor but not SoSD
* You fetch SoSD → again, guaranteed 6 keywords.
* The rest of your draws might add more.
3. You draw both SoSD and a tutor
* You use the tutor to get the next largest set of distinct keywords (like [[Nightveil Predator]] → +3 more distinct keywords).
* That’s a combined 9 unique keywords just from those two creatures—plus potentially more from the rest of the draws.
4. You draw neither
* Then you’re in the “no tutor, no SoSD” baseline scenario, relying on random hits. In practice, that might still yield 5–6 keywords by Turn 4 from the other 28 creatures in your deck.

Because you’re effectively seeing 1–2 more cards by Turn 3 (and similarly by Turn 4), all these probabilities go up:
* Probability(SoSD) is higher.
* Probability(>= 1 tutor) is higher.
* Probability(SoSD and a tutor) is higher (which is the best-case scenario).

## Approximate Expected Values

**Without Early Draw Spells**

Previously, we found:
* Turn 4 expected distinct keywords ≈ 6.25 (after factoring 5 tutors).
* Turn 5 expected distinct keywords ≈ 6.67.

**With +1 to +2 “Extra” Cards by Turn 3**

Because each additional card you see by Turn 3 raises the chance of hitting SoSD or a tutor, your final number of keywords goes up. A rough recalculation (using 11–12 “effective” cards by Turn 3 and 12–13 by Turn 4) might push:
* The “no tutor, no SoSD” baseline from ~5.1–5.4 up to ~5.5–5.8,
* The “tutor or SoSD” scenario from ~7.9–8.3 up to maybe ~8.2–8.6,
* The “tutor and SoSD” scenario (9 guaranteed from SoSD + NP) is also more likely.

Putting it all together with weighted probabilities, you might see an additional +0.3 to +0.7 distinct keywords on average. That yields approximate new totals of:
![Expected Unique Keywords w/ Extra Draws](https://i.ibb.co/gjdqjVK/IMG-5553.jpg)

In other words, once you factor in:
1. The higher odds of tutoring by Turn 3 or Turn 4,
2. The higher odds of simply drawing SoSD early,
3. The possibility of both SoSD and a tutor (which leads to 9+ keywords from two creatures!),

…it’s reasonable to expect around 6.5–7.0 unique keywords by Turn 4 and 7.0–7.5 by Turn 5 on average.

## Final Takeaway

**Key Points**
1. Early draw spells significantly increase your odds of either:
* Hard-drawing [[Sire of Seven Deaths]], or
* Drawing a tutor and using it to find SoSD (or the next best multi-keyword creature if SoSD is already in hand).
2. Distinct Keywords
* If you see only SoSD (or only a tutor), you’ll likely be at 6–8 total keywords by Turn 4.
* If you see both SoSD and a tutor, you can easily hit 9+ distinct keywords with just two creatures.
3. Expected Values
* Previously (no extra draws), we had ~6.25 by Turn 4, ~6.67 by Turn 5.
* Now (with extra draws T1–T3), you can jump to roughly 6.5–7.0 by Turn 4 and 7.0–7.5 by Turn 5 on average, with insane draws that can exceed 9+.

In short, these additional early-card-advantage pieces accelerate the deck’s keyword assembly, making [[Indominus Rex, Alpha]] that much more explosive and consistent.

===endpanel
===endaccordion
===endpanel
===endaccordion
